retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypothyroidism -- Evaluation -- Additional Laboratory Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: 4. Urine iodine concentration: CH may be caused by iodine excess or deficiency (in infants born in an area of endemic goiter or iodine deficiency). Measurement of urine iodine can identify the excess or deficient state. 5. Radiographs of the knees may be obtained at diagnosis. The absence of a lower femoral epiphyseal center indicates severe CH and correlates with later intelligence and motor scores. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: I-123 Uptake -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Neonatal Hypothyroidism
retrieved_docs.content: An iodine organification defect is identified as the cause of neonatal hypothyroidism if RAIU is reduced by at least 10% following the administration of sodium perchlorate. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Iodine Toxicity -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Iodine excess during pregnancy may cause problems for both mother and fetus. Pregnant women may experience subclinical hypothyroidism, with the fetus being affected in different ways. Several case reports in the literature describe problems in the neurological development of the fetus and increased incidence of preterm births. [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Jod-Basedow Syndrome -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Caution is necessary when ordering ICM in pregnant patients due to the ability of iodine to cross the placenta. Exposure to iodine in utero correlates with fetal hypothyroidism and goiter development. Fetal hypothyroidism is thought to be due to the failure of the fetus to escape the physiologic Wolff-Chaikoff effect. Although fetal hypothyroidism after maternal ICM is usually transitory, it can still have detrimental effects on fetal development. Continued exposure of the neonate to iodine in the first few weeks of life may lead to permanent hypothyroidism. [12] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- History and Physical -- Hypothyroidism
retrieved_docs.content: Infants whose mothers experienced hypothyroidism during pregnancy are at risk of hypothyroidism themselves, which can impair cognitive development, possibly leading to mental retardation. In addition, they can develop a compressive goiter, requiring treatment. [7] [8] Observations suggest an increased incidence of jaundice in neonates with hypothyroid mothers during pregnancy and a possible link to hypocalcemia, respiratory distress, urogenital malformations, and cardiovascular abnormalities. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypothyroidism -- Evaluation -- Repeat Screening
retrieved_docs.content: The second screening at two weeks of age or at discharge from the hospital is also recommended for infants with specimen collection within the first 24 hours, multiple births, particularly in cases of same-sex twins (concern for fetal blood mixing). Infants exposed to iodine should be monitored for up to 1 month after exposure to identify iodine-induced hypothyroidism (with low T4 and elevated TSH) following exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypothyroidism -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital hypothyroidism is one of the most common preventable causes of intellectual disability. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of congenital hypothyroidism and highlights the importance of the interprofessional team for accurate interpretation of thyroid function tests to promptly diagnose and treat neonates with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypothyroidism -- Evaluation -- Additional Laboratory Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: 1.2 Thyroid radio nuclear uptake scan: An iodine 123 uptake scan or sodium pertechnetate 99 m, can be used to determine the size and location of the thyroid gland, and to diagnose ectopic thyroid, thyroid aplasia, or hypoplasia. A thyroid scan shows no uptake in patients with thyroid blocking antibodies, and patients with iodine trapping defects will have no uptake on the thyroid scan with a normal or enlarged thyroid on ultrasonography.

retrieved_docs.title: Iodine Toxicity -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: In iodine-sufficient individuals, iodine excess causes elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone levels, which inhibits thyroid hormone production and leads to hypothyroidism and goiter. Patients with iodine deficiency and preexisting thyroid disease may be sensitive to iodine levels that otherwise appear safe for the general population. [18] Neonates, older, and pregnant women may be more susceptible to iodine excess. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypothyroidism -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital hypothyroidism (CH) is defined as thyroid hormone deficiency present at birth. CH must be diagnosed promptly because delay in treatment can lead to irreversible neurological deficits. Before the newborn screening program, CH was one of the most common preventable causes of intellectual disability. Newborn screening (NBS) programs have led to earlier diagnosis and treatment of CH, resulting in improved neurodevelopmental outcomes. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Thyroid Function -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Proper thyroid function is necessary for properly developing the growing brain throughout embryologic development. Both iodine deficiency and congenital hypothyroidism, due to absence, malpositioning, underdevelopment, or failure to make thyroid hormones, can cause fetal hypothyroidism. Hypothyroidism during embryologic development may result in intellectual disability, dwarfism, deafness, and muscle hypertonia.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypothyroidism -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: While these defects cause permanent CH, the condition may also be transient, a result of transplacental passage of maternal anti-thyroid medications (methimazole or propylthiouracil), maternal thyroid blocking antibodies (in a mother with autoimmune thyroid disease), or iodine deficiency or excess. Iodine deficiency, still an important cause of CH worldwide, especially in iodine-poor regions. Newborns exposed to excess iodine (such as iodine-containing antiseptics, or radiographic contrast agents) may develop hypothyroidism as the synthesis of thyroid hormone is transiently inhibited via the Wolff-Chaikoff effect. Central CH is rare and results from a pituitary or hypothalamic abnormality (secondary/tertiary hypothyroidism).

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypothyroidism -- Evaluation -- Additional Laboratory Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: 2. Thyroid autoantibodies: TSH-receptor blocking antibodies may be useful in diagnosing infants with transient CH due to maternal autoimmune thyroid disease, or infants with a sibling with a transient course of CH.

retrieved_docs.title: Pendred Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: A thyroid goiter may appear in late childhood or early adulthood. Most people have normal thyroid levels, but hypothyroidism can happen.

retrieved_docs.title: Iodine Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Iodine is an essential micronutrient that has a crucial role in metabolism. The micronutrient is oxidized to produce iodine-containing thyroid hormones. Thyroid hormones are necessary for regulating growth, metabolism, and many other body functions, especially during fetal and neonatal brain development. [1] Iodine deficiency has long been a recognized global problem and remains a leading cause of preventable fetal brain damage. [2] Iodine deficiency in pregnancy leads to hypothyroidism and impaired infant neurobehavioral development. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Etiology -- Hypothyroidism
retrieved_docs.content: Overt hypothyroidism is defined as a low free T4 (FT4) with high thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) levels. Worldwide, iodine deficiency is the main cause of overt hypothyroidism; in areas where iodine intake is sufficient, the most frequent cause is autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto thyroiditis). [5] [6] Subclinical hypothyroidism is defined as a normal free T4 with high TSH levels and is by far the most frequent thyroid dysfunction occurring in pregnancy. The prevalence depends on the definition of subclinical hypothyroidism, ethnicity, iodine intake, and study design. In most areas, prevalence is between 1.5% and 4.0%. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2661
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy represent a broad category of maternal health issues that include specific conditions such as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before birth), خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل (thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy), and فقر الدم في أثناء الحمل (anemia during pregnancy). Recognizing these sub‑conditions under the umbrella of other complications of pregnancy is important for comprehensive prenatal assessment, as each can affect fetal development and maternal outcomes. Effective screening and management of drug dependence, thyroid abnormalities, and anemia are essential components of obstetric care to reduce risks to both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a broad category that includes several specific maternal health issues. It has the child condition قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (light vomiting of pregnancy) and is classified as encompassing الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery), الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), and خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل (thyroid dysfunction in the gestational period). These various complications highlight the diverse risks that can affect pregnant individuals and underscore the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and appropriate management to protect both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2281
retrieved_docs.content: Mild hyperemesis gravidarum, thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy, and gestational herpes are all specific maternal complications that fall under the broader, uncategorized group of pregnancy complications affecting the mother. Each of these conditions represents a distinct health issue that can arise during gestation and requires appropriate clinical attention despite not being classified within more specific subcategories of maternal pregnancy disorders. Recognizing them as part of the general category of unclassified maternal pregnancy complications helps clinicians consider a wider range of potential problems when evaluating and managing pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2088
retrieved_docs.content: The category “مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” includes several specific maternal pregnancy complications: “الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل” (German measles during pregnancy), “خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل” (thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy), and “فقر الدم في أثناء الحمل” (anemia during pregnancy). In addition, the broader category “مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم” (pregnancy complications affecting the mother) is itself classified under the same unclassified group. This hierarchical structure indicates that both the general set of maternal complications and the particular conditions of German measles, thyroid dysfunction, and anemia in pregnancy are grouped together as pregnancy complications not placed elsewhere, aiding clinicians in comprehensive assessment and management of maternal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 949
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy constitute a broad category that encompasses several specific maternal conditions that are not classified elsewhere. Within this group are drug dependence before delivery (الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة), German measles occurring during pregnancy (الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل), thyroid dysfunction in the gestational period (خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل), mild hyperemesis gravidarum (قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة), herpes infection during pregnancy (الهِربسُ الحَمْلِي), and anemia that develops in pregnancy (فقر الدم في أثناء الحمل). Each of these conditions is considered a child of the overarching classification “مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (pregnancy complications affecting the mother, not classified elsewhere). By grouping these diverse disorders under the umbrella of other complications of pregnancy, clinicians are reminded to assess for and manage these less‑common but clinically important maternal issues, ensuring comprehensive prenatal care and appropriate intervention when they arise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 786
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal pregnancy complications that are not classified elsewhere include several distinct conditions. Mild hyperemesis gravidarum (قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة) represents a less severe form of pregnancy‑related nausea and vomiting. German measles occurring during pregnancy (الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل) poses a risk to both mother and fetus and requires careful monitoring. Thyroid dysfunction in the gestational period (خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل) can affect maternal metabolism and fetal development, necessitating endocrine evaluation and treatment. Gestational herpes infection (الهِربسُ الحَمْلِي) is another maternal complication that may lead to adverse outcomes if not managed appropriately. Finally, anemia during pregnancy (فقر الدم في أثناء الحمل) is a common hematologic disorder that can compromise maternal health and fetal growth. Together, these conditions constitute a category of pregnancy‑related maternal complications that are recognized but not placed within more specific classification groups, highlighting the need for individualized assessment and management in prenatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is classified as a type of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complication). Recognizing that fetal disorders are encompassed within the broader category of pregnancy complications helps clinicians identify, monitor, and manage these issues during prenatal care, ensuring appropriate assessment and intervention for both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: Conditions of fetal growth and malnutrition are regarded as a parent category that includes the specific outcomes of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. The grouping of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation serves as a classification label for these adverse perinatal conditions, emphasizing their interrelated nature in the context of impaired fetal development and inadequate nutrition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2809
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with several obstetric conditions, including gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل), threatened miscarriage (تهديد بالإجهاض), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد). These associations highlight the importance of evaluating and managing metabolic, hematologic, and early‑pregnancy complications when counseling patients about elective pregnancy termination, ensuring comprehensive risk assessment and optimal maternal health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is classified as a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that these health issues are primarily associated with the gestational period and its immediate aftermath.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1836
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal pregnancy complications, not classified elsewhere (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر) is the overarching category that encompasses several more specific conditions. Within this parent classification are the child entities thyroid dysfunction in pregnancy (خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل) and gestational herpes (الهِربسُ الحَمْلِي), indicating that both of these disorders are considered types of maternal pregnancy complications that do not fit into other specific categories. In addition, the broader group labeled maternal pregnancy complications (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم) is itself a child of the same parent category, showing that the general classification of maternal complications and the particular conditions of thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy and gestational herpes are all organized under the umbrella of complications that are not otherwise classified. This hierarchical arrangement clarifies how these specific and general maternal health issues are related for medical taxonomy and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.
